name: Shanxi Jinzhong mid-highlands (China, ~850m)

staple_foods.name: Golden Delicious apple

primary_metabolites.fructose_concentration: 5.0-6.5 g/100g [Jinzhong highland apple — intermediate between Yuncheng lowland RSU-13 and Linfen highland RSU-14]

organic_acids.malic_acid: 0.43-0.47 g/100g [Golden Delicious, Jinzhong 800-1000m — Li et al. 2021 Foods 10:2950; 4.52±0.20 mg/g FW; same study as RSU-13 Yuncheng and RSU-14 Linfen — directly comparable units]

staple_foods.notes: Golden Delicious apple grown in Jinzhong City, Shanxi Province, at 800-1000m (RSU centroid 850m). Li et al. (2021, Foods 10:2950) — the same study that underpins RSU-13 (Yuncheng, 600m, malic 0.22-0.28) and RSU-14 (Linfen, 1050m, malic 0.48-0.53) — measured Jinzhong at 4.52±0.20 mg/g FW (0.452 g/100g). This is the critical interpolation point in the Shanxi apple altitude gradient, filling the 600-1050m gap. The units, analytical method (HPLC), and cultivar are identical to RSU-13 and RSU-14, making this the highest-confidence new data point in the search. Malic acid range 0.43-0.47 g/100g uses ±1SD from the reported mean.
data_sources: Li et al. (2021) — Effects of genetic background and altitude on sugars, malic acid and ascorbic acid in fruits of wild and cultivated apples, Foods 10(12):2950

staple_foods.name: millet (Shanxi highland)
staple_foods.notes: Millet is a highland Shanxi staple alongside apple.

notes: Jinzhong City, Shanxi Province, China — mid-altitude apple growing zone at 800-1000m (centroid 850m). Loess plateau sedimentary soils, semi-arid continental climate with high temperature seasonality. This RSU is the critical interpolation point in the Shanxi apple altitude natural experiment (RSU-13 at 600m, RSU-55 at 850m, RSU-14 at 1050m — all same paper, same cultivar, same analytical method). Adding this RSU increases the apple malic acid regression from n=4 to n=5 with a directly within-study data point, substantially improving LOOCV stability.
